item.title: Re: Neat, but needs more meat
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough, but the length of it already kind of stretches belief considering that it was scribbled on a wall while people were supposed to be cleaning. Whatever does (or doesn’t) float your boat though.

Anyway, I’m confident that the lamp is not an actual existing SCP. If it were, it would’ve been linked.

item.title: Re: Neat, but needs more meat
item.content:encoded:
No, I get that. But that's just an idea. That's a skeleton. Without something engaging about the object itself, it didn't have enough going for it besides the format screw, and it doesn't do enough with that concept (i.e testing logs, or more varied perspectives).
Ultimately I'd like to see the concept fleshed out more. Maybe even a tale from the perspective of someone experiencing this. Skips where the object itself isn't the point really benefit from that kind of thing.

I get the point, I just think it could do more, and without doing more, it feels like the kind of thing where I'd want to see someone take the idea further, of D-class containing something dangerous on their own.

D-bois containing an anomaly that would be infohazardous is neat, but… It needs more meat.

Also, I know the lamp isn't relevant. But it not being hyperlinked just makes me unsure if it's an existing SCP or one that exists for this narrative.

item.title: Re: Neat, but needs more meat
item.content:encoded: I think you might’ve missed the point. The actual anomaly isn’t the point, and the lamp definitely isn’t. This is a story about some people who are usually treated as worthless, disposable un-persons by the vast faceless juggernaut of the SCP Foundation acquiring the power to destroy the world, but instead of unleashing it on their captors they secretly carry on the mission of the Foundation, secretly saving the world once a month. This is about the secret lives of the people whose lives are worthless, and the power that even the most powerless of people can hold.

item.title: Neat, but needs more meat
item.content:encoded:
Not really any point to adding my two cents to a four year old article, but I like the concept but it feels like it doesn't… go anywhere.
I would have liked more graffiti that felt like testing logs. More description of how the Ds deal with this voice. The hidden text being hidden all the way in the source is also a bit much. I never would have even noticed that if someone else hadn't mentioned it, and it doesn't… add much. It doesn't go anywhere.
I like the format screw, even with how ridiculous it is that an entire wall is scratched into with this long ass article, I just wish it was better used. This is such a good idea, but "mind parasite that wants to enslave humanity" just feels meh. I also hate the whole "D-Class are executed at the end of the month" trope.

Basically the format is good and I love the imagery, but the SCP itself is a bit bland.
I also don't know which SCP this light in question is. Is it not an existing one, or what?

item.title: D-Class Contain Memetic SCP
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I'll admit. That's really a cool idea of the D-Class doing their own version of a SCP file, combined with the fear of what may actually happen if The Foundation do get their hands on this.

I'll give it a +1, but I was kinda expecting strange whispers within a few minutes that slowly grow louder and louder. It would totally change and it would be somewhat unexpected. Keep up the good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit to the article has been reverted.

This article is not tagged as "collaborative." Please do not make unauthorized edits to the article without explicit permission from the author. If you have indeed received permission, please note as such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I already liked this skip to begin with, but the extra text only viewable in the page source just adds an extra layer of brilliance to it. It's like the collective will of all the D-class who contain it make it so the skip can't even make itself known to the Foundation or the audience. I'm loving this even more now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i know the account was deleted but it seems like you just skimed over the artical. so for you if you ever come back here and for anyone else with the same questions:

1. its a infohazard parasite whos goal is to enslave humanity
2. it landed in a D-class who was smart enough to know that this needs to be contained and enough understanding of the foundation to know if a researcher knew about it it couldnt be contained
3. likely to have been writen by multiple D-class over multiple cleanings. also the only place with no cameras and only D class are allowed to go in
4. again, its a infohazard parasite. the D-class have just managed to contain it within the D-class ranks.
5. what we have here is a list of stuff in the SCP universe. in the SCP universe this dosnt actualy exist on the records. the D-class just bump up the number whenever the current one is taken.
6. if i am correct D-class are sometimes allowed to read a heavly redacted version of documents if the situation calls for it. while the classification is wrong and should be elucid due to the fact that it can easly be contained if the proper circumstances are met there is a valid reason for it: to the D-class that wrote it they likely thought that keter just means dangerous. and not hard to contain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to have to downvote this one, I'm afraid.

I'm torn in the premise. I think it appears as a reasonably good idea, but it's not clear to me what the SCP actually is. It's memetic, that much I understand - but:

  1. What does it do?
  2. Why have the Foundation not discovered it yet?
  3. How did text this length get scratched into a wall without no-one noticing?
  4. Why does it affect only D-Class?
  5. If the Foundation have not discovered this, why is it in the main database?
  6. How do the D-Class have enough knowledge to format this like an official SCP document? In-universe, D-Class would not have access to or awareness of this format.

I think if the premise was hinted at a bit more, I would like this. But in my opinion, this would be more suited as a tale rather than an SCP. It just doesn't fit in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D-bois have taken over

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay when they say "the lamp," "no cameras," "don't cover it," "mop," and "read it while the other 2 mop" (which means 3 people) I can only think of 173, but then again you can't really have two people mopping if you have to stare at it. What SCP was it referencing (assuming it wasn't a "SCP-████" for when it references something that doesn't exist)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My reaction: "WTF IS THIS KETER SHIT"
My 2nd Reaction: "AND WHY TF DO I LIKE THIS"
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how it starts out like a -j, but then slowly goes downhill into the horror zone. very spooky.
+1 from me.

item.title: Lamp?
item.content:encoded:
Can anyone actually find the lamp this is written in?

Someone posted a lamp, but after reading it, it didn't seem like the one the page was talking about.

I NEED MY GOOP LAMP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have never not read that as PANDA FORCE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's probably not this lamp, since it doesn't create goop that needs clean-up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this article isn't actually in the database, right? It's all scratched into the wall? In that case, what does it even mean to have invisible words scratched into the wall?

Anyway, I liked this article, although I thought the D-class tone was a little cliche, like a cross between 1930s gangsters and 1990s gangstas.

My thought while reading the article was, basically, is there really an anomaly involved here? Or is it just the power of suggestion on people in a high-stress situation? The only things they know about this anomaly were handed down through a self-authoritative source that gains credence by appealing to the "us versus them" mentality of the D-class who clean the lamp room. The only specific effects the anomaly is claimed to have are auditory hallucinations, and it's reasonable to think that some small percentage of the D-class (being criminals) will already be schizophrenic and prone to auditory hallucinations anyway. So there will always be a few D-class telling the rest of them that the stuff the wall says is all true, and there will be others who succumb to that suggestion and start hearing voices as well, induced by high stress levels and probable sleep deprivation.

In fact, I think it's almost guaranteed that if the D-class ever did mention this to the researchers, nobody would believe them, and they'd probably fast track the D-class in question for termination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pandora Force sounds like an anime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely necessary format screw. Beautiful characterization. They didn't choose the Redpill PANDORA FORCE D-Class life, the D-Class life chose them.

And utterly, coldly beleivable.

Upvoted before I found out about the 0-font madness. I regret that I have but one upvote to give for my Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Since I only ever read on mobile I can't look at source)

There is nothing about mobile browsers that should stop you from looking at the source. I read this site on Chrome on my Android all the time (doing so right now) and Options->Page Source works just fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Besides, they're D-Class aka ex-convicts. So it's expected for a lot of them to act out or flat out purposely breach containment, e.t.c. sometimes. As for how they keep it hidden, it's because a lot of them are murderous psychopaths taken from death row, soooo… they don't have the best of track records when it comes to behavior. Which is why murdering anyone who you think is going to snitch wouldn't draw much attention aside from you being termin- ahem released to a better place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're better off going to "+ Options" and hitting "Page Source". That'll let you peek under the hood without blocking anyone else from editing, and without the possibility of accidentally editing the page. (yes, this has happened before)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god…

In this case it size zero text, like many articles that use this gimmick.

Now I have a barely restrained urge to reread the 50 scps or so that I feel like may have done this. I've already gotten into the habit of hitting edit (Since I only ever read on mobile I can't look at source) just to check for hidden stuff, but reread would take way too long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The lamp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The farting sloths incident?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love to think of some researchers sitting there watching all of this unfold in order to assess the response of D-Class upon exposure to a really simple Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Options->Page Source will tell you.

In this case it size zero text, like many articles that use this gimmick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The real anomaly is hiding that text in plain sight. What font size is it? Font size -1?

To be honest, I need to find out how they did that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PANDORA FORCE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the evil squid thing is trying to bargain with the reader, hah. HAH.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
no one sees me anymore

not since

the incident…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the farting sloths, it's Sorts. Long time no see, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, I love the defiance. "Hey maybe this is redemption? NAH IT'S AN EFF YOU. THIS ONE IS OURS."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This…this…is amazing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is safer, and generally as effective, to view the source: +Options->Page Source. Opening an Edit window might result in accidental edits, and also puts a lock on the page, blocking people who might have a legitimate edit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When you want to know how something is done on a page, click 'edit' and examine the source code. Doing that on this page will show you that there are various passages whose font size has been set to 0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How the hell did they do that with the copy-paste? It's not invisitext …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super cool, I especially liked the little things in the margins. How'd you get it to add extra text when you try to copy/paste parts of the article? I tried to paste "refer to containment procedures first paragraph" and got an extra "I WILL CRUSH THOSE WHO OPPRESS YOU." added in, which was thoroughly creepy. Once again, kudos.

item.title: Hmm..
item.content:encoded: So the staff sends only D-Class into the room to clean goop off a lamp. They clearly know at least that the lamp is dangerous for some reason. Either they don't know about this particular entity, or they just think that the lamp goop causes people to go insane and die or something instead of this entity, which is enough to protect them from it. Either that, or one staff member is designated to know about it and takes countermemes or something to last long enough that replacement staff isn't required too often.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my opinion, this really embodied the spirit of the DClass and is a worthy winner of DCon. I loved everything, from the insults to the reasherchers to the implication that the artical was finished only after multiple DClass had read and added to it, to the true feeling of hated and spite you that was palpable through the whole thing, to the incredible format screw. Loved it. Loved all of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just in case you’re the eldritch thing: fuck off squidface. We’re worthless sure, but we can get shit done just as well as the others. You’re not getting in LET ME IN.

+1 for reference to the poor God who wants the cults with fetishes to stop annoying him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A worthy (joint) winner of the contest. So many of the entries attempted to "explain" the use of D-Class (either as an anomaly or by giving a "realistic" alternative justification), or to humanise individual D-Class by providing backstory, but this is one of the very few entries that provides D-Class with agency and purpose. It also gives them a distinct voice, and the commitment to that approach is what sells this idea - a half-hearted attempt would have backfired.

I agree with other comments that the second and third paragraphs of the description lose some impact, as you are caught between D-Class tone and actually getting some information across, but the ending restores that balance nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ipme, it's fine if someone doesn't like an SCP article and doesn't want to expand on it. Please don't be antagonistic towards people in the discussion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Unique entry with the possibility to crash and burn but executed well, beats the clustertruck over-the-top apocalypse stuff any day. It's great that the D-Class have finally got a win, I've always thought they've worked hard to deserve some victory :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's just something so academic about your critique, just the way you put it. Especially the way how it offers a constructive method towards the author, backed with well-defined complaints and figures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just in case you’re the eldritch thing: fuck off squidface

If I could upvote individual text this would be it. but anyways, this is by far one of my favorite articles on the site, due to how it strays from the norm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the concept enough to +1 just for that, and the execution is solid enough that it doesn't remove the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slightly old post, but:

The MTF member wrote the SCP report before killing himself, and the D-Class encountered it and used the information to kill 1983.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a real nice addition to the site. The concept is very clever and the speaker's voice is highly engaging. I liked a lot of the little bits of graffitit intermixed with the main writing, that was a really nice touch. +1
Technically +103, but great job!

item.title: Re: http://yugioh.wikia.com/wiki/Dicelops
item.content:encoded:
+ Show opinion post
- Hide click HERE to close

This one’s pretty neato. I’m not sure if I’d ever seen an SCP written quite like this (scratched on a wall), though I feel the general gist of this has been done before (D-Class only knowledge). Where, I’m not sure. But I like how it’s done here. Using a random throwaway SCP to actually host the real thing in-universe.

I do wish it had a lot more… scattered graffiti. Partway through it just doesn’t feel as… casual? As it does early on. There’s a definite different feel between the beginning/end and the middling chunk. Maybe it’s intentional, I'm not sure.

Pretty excellent SCP though. A+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A pretty good example of the format screw arising because the story required it, and not because it was imposed for its own sake and then details justifying it later (maybe) added in later.

I'm reminded of the tale in this same vein that also showed up in the contest. In this instance, though, the singular speaker and the sense of urgency helped give this some focus that made it feel a little tighter, and a little clearer in what it was conveying.

Overall, nice work, and a good embodiment of the contest parameters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favourite in the contest so far. +1

item.title: @undergroundmonorail
item.content:encoded:
Why do they feel the need to use an unoccupied number? I get that there are reasons on this side of the fourth wall why the skip needs a number and there can't be a conflict, but why do the characters care? Someone even wrote "y dont we just leave it" and I can't think of a satisfying answer.

To me, this means that some D-Class who know about this want to do things by the book, just as the Foundation does for the other SCPs; and some others don't give a shit as long as they can dream that this will avenge them someday.
I'd like to have the author's take on this though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic. The more I read it, the more fantastic it becomes.

I found the hidden text, but I wonder if it's not better without it - one can wonder if this particular, special SCP exists at all, or if it is something the D-Class invented/imagined/believe in just to go 'AHA, Foundation, we got you by the balls!'.

Format screw, goes all over the place, no clinical tone, added scribblings here and there - just great. Something like a 'liberated SCP article'!
My favourite in the contest by far, and now one of my top five favourites in the mainlist also.

Good job, Mr Ike! <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seeing this made me go back for a second look; there is indeed a hidden text thread within this SCP. I'll leave it to individuals to go in and find it, but it certainly adds an interesting layer…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really, really great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Huh. Wow.

If this were written straight, it'd be a laughable example of everything not to do. "It's a voice what gets into your head and does…something, it's not actually explained what, but it's evil." But in the form it's in, I daresay it works. And I love the perspective. It's like if we suddenly found out that, say, birds have been keeping a deadly secret from us for generations. (And the graffiti from those D-class who didn't take it so seriously is a fun touch reminiscent of the now-taboo notes from staff in older articles.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If all the D-Class stuck to the script and didn't act out, then it's likely that the Foundation has nothing to suspect in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is maybe one of the more triumphant articles on the site, and definitely for the contest. It's up there among my favorites.
